title: PowerShell Credential Management - Key Vault, SecretManagement, And Secure Storage Patterns
applies_to: Storing API tokens and passwords securely for PowerShell automation
Retrieving credentials at runtime without hardcoding in scripts
Azure Key Vault integration from PowerShell scripts and runbooks
Managed Identity as a zero-credential auth model for Azure-hosted scripts

confirmed_facts.fact: Never hardcode credentials in scripts. Use environment variables, encrypted vaults, or Managed Identity. For Qualys and BitSight API tokens, store in Azure Key Vault and retrieve at runtime. Hardcoded secrets in scripts are exposed in source control, logs, and process memory.

Azure Key Vault retrieval using Az.KeyVault module: `$secret = Get-AzKeyVaultSecret -VaultName $vaultName -Name $secretName -AsPlainText`. Requires the script context to be authenticated to Azure via `Connect-AzAccount`, a Managed Identity, or a service principal. The `-AsPlainText` flag returns a plain string instead of a SecureString.

Microsoft.PowerShell.SecretManagement provides a vendor-neutral vault interface. Install the framework and a vault extension: `Install-Module Microsoft.PowerShell.SecretManagement; Install-Module SecretManagement.AzureKeyVault`. Register vault: `Register-SecretVault -Name 'MyVault' -ModuleName SecretManagement.AzureKeyVault -VaultParameters @{ AZKVaultName='my-vault'; SubscriptionId='...' }`. Retrieve: `Get-Secret -Name 'QualysPassword' -Vault 'MyVault' -AsPlainText`.

Azure Automation Account encrypted variables (no Key Vault required): `New-AzAutomationVariable -AutomationAccountName $aa -ResourceGroupName $rg -Name 'QualysPassword' -Value $pw -Encrypted $true`. Retrieve in runbook: `$pw = Get-AutomationVariable -Name 'QualysPassword'`. Encrypted variables are stored and transmitted encrypted; value is never exposed in Azure Portal.

DPAPI-based encrypted credential file (Windows only, same user/machine): `$secureString = ConvertTo-SecureString $plainTextSecret -AsPlainText -Force; ConvertFrom-SecureString $secureString | Out-File 'credential.dat'`. Decrypt: `$secureString = Get-Content 'credential.dat' | ConvertTo-SecureString`. Only the same Windows user on the same machine can decrypt. Not suitable for multi-user or cloud-hosted scripts.

Managed Identity (zero-credential model for Azure-hosted compute): when the script runs on an Azure VM, Azure Automation Account, Azure Function, or AKS pod with a Managed Identity, authenticate with `Connect-AzAccount -Identity` or `Connect-MgGraph -Identity`. No secrets to store, rotate, or leak. Preferred for all Azure-hosted automation.

Service principal authentication using a certificate (preferred over client secret for long-lived automation): `Connect-AzAccount -ServicePrincipal -TenantId $tenantId -ApplicationId $appId -CertificateThumbprint $thumbprint`. The certificate private key must be in the local machine certificate store.

Never pass plain-text secrets via Write-Host, Write-Verbose, Write-Debug, or transcript files. When logging API responses for debugging, sanitize by removing Authorization headers and any fields that contain secret values before writing to output streams.

Convert a SecureString to plain text at the last moment before use (e.g., Base64 encoding for Basic Auth): `$plain = [System.Runtime.InteropServices.Marshal]::PtrToStringAuto([System.Runtime.InteropServices.Marshal]::SecureStringToBSTR($secureString))`. This avoids -AsPlainText for intermediate handling. Release the BSTR pointer after use.

implementation_notes: Establish a single `Get-Credentials` function at the top of each script that retrieves all required secrets (Qualys password, BitSight token, Graph client secret) from the vault in one pass, then passes them as parameters to worker functions. Worker functions should accept SecureString or plain strings as parameters, never call the vault themselves.

For local development and testing, use environment variables (`$env:QUALYS_PASSWORD`) set in the developer's shell session rather than checked-in secrets. Add a `.env` file to `.gitignore` and document required variable names in README.
Use Managed Identity whenever the script runs in Azure. This eliminates credential rotation overhead entirely and is the most secure option.

Rotate secrets on a documented schedule. Key Vault supports versioned secrets; update the secret value in Key Vault without changing the script. Use Key Vault access policies or RBAC to limit which identities can read each secret.
caveats: DPAPI-encrypted credential files are non-portable: a file encrypted by User A cannot be decrypted by User B or by the same user on a different machine. Do not use this pattern for service accounts or Azure-hosted scripts.
Az.KeyVault requires an Azure subscription context established via Connect-AzAccount or Managed Identity before Get-AzKeyVaultSecret will succeed.
SecretManagement.AzureKeyVault vault extension may require interactive login for the first registration. For unattended setups, use the Az.KeyVault module directly.

Automation Account encrypted variables are encrypted at rest but transmitted to the runbook as plaintext in memory. They are not a Key Vault replacement but are sufficient for low-to-medium sensitivity secrets.
